message.content:
## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** Competitive landscape and market analysis for AI-powered recruitment platforms targeting solo/independent recruiters and small recruiting agencies (teams of 5-10), with a freemium model (free individual tier + paid team tier).

Research these dimensions specifically:
1. Existing recruitment platforms (ATS, recruiting CRM, applicant tracking) that target solo recruiters and small agencies — e.g. Recruit CRM, Loxo, Crelate, Manatal, JobAdder, Zoho Recruit, Bullhorn, RecruiterFlow, Vincere, iSmartRecruit, Ceipal, Tracker, Hireflow, hiring tools like Workable, Ashby, Greenhouse (note which are NOT for small/solo). For each relevant one: target customer, pricing/tiers, free tier availability, AI features, key strengths/weaknesses.
2. Which of these use a freemium or free-tier model (Zoho Recruit free plan, etc.) and how they monetize. How "easy to start" / self-serve onboarding is positioned.
3. Where AI is being applied in recruiting today (resume parsing, candidate matching/ranking, sourcing, outreach email generation, interview scheduling, candidate Q&A, job description writing, chrome extensions for sourcing from LinkedIn). Which features are table-stakes vs differentiators in 2025-2026.
4. The pain points of small/independent recruiters: data scattered across WhatsApp/Excel/Google Drive, time spent on admin, lack of context on past placements, no revenue visibility. What tools claim to solve these and gaps that remain.
5. Pricing benchmarks: typical per-seat/month pricing for recruiting CRM/ATS at the small-agency level, and what free tiers include.
6. Market size and trends for recruitment software / recruiting CRM, especially the SMB / independent recruiter segment.

18 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] Ashby positions itself as enterprise recruiting software targeting large organizations, NOT solo recruiters or small agencies.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://www.ashbyhq.com/enterprise (primary)
Quote: "Enterprise-grade recruiting software you actually want to use ... Built to support large organizations with sophisticated hiring needs."
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is verbatim-supported and independently corroborated. Ashby's own /enterprise page reads "Enterprise-grade recruiting software you actually want to use" and "Built to support large organizations with sophisticated hiring needs," with a "Why Enterprise Companies Choose Ashby" section and logos like Shopify and Snowflake. Independent reviews (pin.com, costbench, research.com via WebSearch June 2026) confirm Ashby's smallest tier (Foundations) starts at ~$400/month for orgs "up to 100 employees," with Plus (101-1,000) and Enterprise (1,000+) tiers — all priced/structured by company size, roughly double Greenhouse/Lever, making it explicitly unsuitable for solo recruiters or 5-10 person agencies. Minor caveat: Ashby also targets startups/scale-ups, not only giant enterprises, but this does not contradict the claim's core assertion that it is NOT for solo/small-agency recruiters. Source is primary (Ashby's own site) and corroborating reviews are current (2026).

### [1] Loxo offers a permanently free tier ($0 forever) for 1 user that includes an ATS, Recruiting CRM, unlimited jobs/projects, and a Chrome extension.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.loxo.co/pricing (primary)
Quote: "Free - Cost: $0 forever - Users: 1 user - Includes: ATS, Recruiting CRM, Loxo Boost, unlimited jobs/projects, Chrome extension, optional contact info access"
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source (loxo.co/pricing) directly confirms a "Free Tier ($0 Forever)" for "1 user" including: Applicant Tracking System (ATS), Recruiting CRM, Loxo Boost, "Unlimited jobs/projects", Chrome extension/importing, and optional access to personal contact information. This matches the claim and supporting quote nearly verbatim. Corroborated by multiple independent 2026 sources: G2 (g2.com/products/loxo/pricing), iSmartRecruit (ismartrecruit.com/tools/loxo), Pin (pin.com/blog/loxo-pricing), and SaaSworthy — all describe a "Free Forever" tier with ATS, Recruiting CRM, Loxo Boost, unlimited jobs, and Chrome extension for 1 user. No contradicting evidence found. Source quality (vendor primary + multiple secondary reviews) matches claim strength; claim is current as of 2026.

### [2] Loxo's paid Basic plan costs $169 per user/month (annual pricing) and adds Sales CRM, multiple users, analytics, and resume parsing on top of the free tier.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.loxo.co/pricing (primary)
Quote: "Basic - Cost: $169 per user/month (annual pricing) - Adds to Free: Sales CRM, job board posting, multiple users, custom dashboards, analytics, resume parsing, technical support, reporting"
Verifier evidence (high): Verified against the primary source (https://www.loxo.co/pricing) via WebFetch. The page lists the Basic plan at "$169 Per user/month" with annual billing, exactly as claimed. Features added to Free in the Basic tier include: Sales CRM, organic job board posting, multiple users, custom dashboards, analytics, resume parsing, technical support, and reporting. The claim's four cited features (Sales CRM, multiple users, analytics, resume parsing) are all present and accurately attributed as additions on top of the Free tier. WebSearch corroborated: multiple third-party sources (Pin, GLOZO titled "Is $169/User Worth It?", iSmartRecruit) reference the $169/user figure as Loxo's published Basic rate. One third-party site (augtal) cited $119 and another $109, but these likely reflect annual-discount variants or stale data; the $169 figure is confirmed directly from the cited primary source. Claim is current (2026 pricing), well-supported by the quote, and source quality (vendor's own pricing page) matches the strength of the factual pricing claim.

### [3] Loxo embeds AI features including Natural Language Search, an AI Notetaker, AI agents (Self-Updating CRM Agent, Contact-Info Finding Agent, Candidate Submittal Agent), AI Job Matching, and Loxo Outreach GPT.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.loxo.co/pricing (primary)
Quote: "Natural Language Search, AI Notetaker, AI agents (Self-Updating CRM Agent, Contact-Info Finding Agent, Candidate Submittal Agent), AI Job Matching, and Loxo Outreach GPT are emphasized across tiers."
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (loxo.co/pricing) confirms all named features verbatim: Natural Language Search ("Start your candidate search with plain text"), AI Notetaker ("Auto-capture and sync interview notes"), three AI agents named exactly — Self-Updating CRM Agent, Contact-Info Finding Agent, Candidate Submittal Agent — plus AI Job Matching ("See which jobs are the best fit for candidates") and Loxo Outreach. Corroborated by independent sources: PRWeb/StaffingHub/TechRSeries press releases on Natural Language Search (2025 launch), Loxo's dedicated AI Notetaker product page (loxo.co/ai-agents/ai-notetaker), and the loxo.co/products/ai page. Outreach GPT confirmed as crafting "personalized emails and drip campaigns." Features are current (2025-2026). This is a factual product-feature description, not an extraordinary claim, and the vendor's own pricing page is the appropriate authoritative source for what features it offers. The only minor weakness is the phrase "Loxo Outreach GPT" — the pricing page lists it as "Loxo Outreach" while "Outreach GPT" appears in other Loxo materials; this is a naming nuance, not a refutation, as the feature unambiguously exists.

### [4] Zoho Recruit offers a free-forever plan limited to one active job with 256MB storage, and its paid tiers start at $25/recruiter per month.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.selectsoftwarereviews.com/buyer-guide/free-recruiting-software (secondary)
Quote: "Zoho Recruit ... Free Offering: Free-forever plan for one active job; Paid Tier: Starts at $25/recruiter per month; Storage: 256MB limit on free tier"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is well-supported and current. Zoho's OFFICIAL pricing page (zoho.com/recruit/pricing.html) confirms a "Forever Free" plan with "1 active job/recruiter license." Three independent secondary sources corroborate the specifics: pin.com (2026) states free plan = "1 active job" + "256MB of storage" and Standard paid tier "begins at $25/user/month"; avahr.com and SelectSoftware Reviews independently report the same 256MB / 1-job / $25 figures. The supporting quote ($25/recruiter/mo start, 256MB free-tier storage, 1 active job free-forever) matches the claim exactly with no overreach. Sources are 2025-2026 dated and include the primary vendor. Minor non-material note: official page lists 3 tiers (Free/Standard $25/Enterprise) while some reviewers cite a $50 Professional tier too — irrelevant to this claim which only concerns the free limits and $25 starting price.

### [5] Recruit CRM targets small employment agencies and starts at $85/month per user, offering a free trial (not a free-forever plan) limited to one sequence and one hiring pipeline.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.selectsoftwarereviews.com/buyer-guide/free-recruiting-software (secondary)
Quote: "Recruit CRM ... Paid Tier: Starts at $85/month per user ... Target: Small employment agencies ... Limitation: Limited to one sequence and hiring pipeline on free tier"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by primary and secondary sources. (1) PRICING: Recruit CRM's official pricing page and TrustRadius/multiple aggregators confirm the entry "Pro" tier is $85/user/month (TrustRadius: Pro $85, Business $125, Enterprise $165). Minor variance exists (Capterra lists Pro at $100, some sources cite $109), but $85 is the most commonly cited entry price and matches the claim's "starts at $85." (2) FREE TRIAL NOT FREE-FOREVER: Confirmed by the official site itself — "Unlike other recruiting software, Recruit CRM provides an unlimited free trial" with "certain usage limits" and "no credit card required." It is explicitly a trial model, not a freemium/free-forever plan, exactly as the claim states. (3) ONE SEQUENCE / ONE PIPELINE: Confirmed directly on recruitcrm.io/pricing — the Pro plan is "Limited to 1 sequence" and "1 pipeline," while Business/Enterprise offer unlimited sequences and multiple pipelines. (4) TARGET: Small employment/recruiting agencies is consistent with Recruit CRM's positioning. NUANCE: The SSR secondary source attributes the "1 sequence / 1 pipeline" limit to a "free tier," but Recruit CRM has no free-forever tier — the limit actually applies to the entry-level Pro paid plan (and by extension whatever the trial exposes). This is a slight framing imprecision in the source, but the substantive facts (entry price $85, trial not freemium, 1 sequence + 1 pipeline at entry level) all check out against the vendor's own current 2026 pricing page. Not outdated, not marketing fluff, corroborated by primary source.

### [6] Manatal targets SMBs and offers only a 14-day trial (no free-forever plan) with paid tiers starting at $15/month per user, including AI-powered candidate filtering and recommendations.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.selectsoftwarereviews.com/buyer-guide/free-recruiting-software (secondary)
Quote: "Manatal ... Free Offering: 14-day trial (no credit card required); Paid Tier: Starts at $15/month per user; AI Features: "AI-powered candidate filtering and recommendations" ... Limitation: No free-forever plan available"
Verifier evidence (high): Manatal's own pricing page (manatal.com/pricing, primary source) confirms every element of the claim: (1) "14-day free trial with no credit card required" and explicitly "no free-forever plan option"; (2) cheapest paid tier is the Professional Plan at $15/user/month (annual billing); (3) AI features built into all plans including "AI Recommendations" and "AI Candidate Enrichment." Multiple independent secondary sources corroborate (G2, TrustRadius, ITQlick, peoplemanagingpeople), all citing Professional $15, Enterprise $35, Enterprise Plus $55 per user/month. The only minor nuance is that $15 is the annual-billing rate (monthly billing is $19), but "starting at $15/month per user" is the standard, accurate representation of the entry price and not misleading. SMB targeting is consistent with Manatal's positioning. Claim is current (2026 pricing matches) and well-supported by a primary source.

### [7] Recruiting software pricing for small businesses (under 50 employees) ranges roughly $15-$200 per user per month, with Manatal the cheapest entry at $15/mo and Loxo at $119/mo.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.selectsoftwarereviews.com/blog/recruitment-software-pricing (secondary)
Quote: "Small businesses (under 50 employees): $15–$200/user/month"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported and current (2026). The cited SelectSoftwareReviews page confirms the "$15-$200/user/month" range for small businesses under 50 employees and explicitly names Manatal at $15/mo and Loxo at $119/mo. Both figures are independently corroborated by other 2026 sources: (1) Manatal $15/user/mo is verified by G2, TrustRadius and augtal as the cheapest published tier (annual Professional billing; monthly is $19). (2) Loxo $119/user/mo is verified by augtal's 2026 pricing breakdown as the Basic plan entry price (range $119-$169/user/mo; Loxo also has a free 1-user tier). Minor caveat: "$15 is the cheapest entry" reflects annual billing, and some competitors may price lower, but this does not contradict the stated $15-$200 range. Source quality (multiple independent pricing aggregators plus the named secondary source) matches the modest strength of this pricing-range claim.

### [8] Freemium is one of six recognized pricing models in recruitment software, alongside subscription, pay-per-user, tiered, custom, and open-source; free tiers exist among lower-cost ATS/CRM tools (Zoho Recruit, Recruit CRM, Loxo, Breezy HR).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.selectsoftwarereviews.com/blog/recruitment-software-pricing (secondary)
Quote: "The document describes six primary models: subscription-based, pay-per-user, tiered, custom, freemium, and open-source."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by the primary source and corroborated. WebFetch of the SSR article (selectsoftwarereviews.com/blog/recruitment-software-pricing, updated for 2026) confirms it names exactly six pricing models: (1) subscription-based, (2) pay-per-user, (3) tiered, (4) custom, (5) free/open-source, (6) freemium. This matches the claim verbatim. Independent WebSearch corroborated the same taxonomy (Software Advice, People Managing People, Manatal all describe subscription/tiered/pay-per-user/freemium/open-source models). The freemium vendor examples are also verified: SSR states Zoho Recruit "adopts a freemium model"; separate searches confirm Loxo offers a "Free Forever" tier (1 user, core ATS/CRM, Chrome extension), and Recruit CRM and Breezy HR are listed with free plans. The source is a current (2026) reputable software-review aggregator, and this is a descriptive/taxonomic claim of modest strength, not an extraordinary one requiring a primary academic source. No contradicting evidence found. One minor caveat: pricing-model "taxonomy" is informal and not a formal industry standard, but the claim accurately reflects what the cited source states, so it is not an overreach.

### [9] The global recruitment software market was valued at USD 3.41 billion in 2025 and is projected to reach USD 7.48 billion by 2034.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/industry-reports/recruitment-software-market-100081 (secondary)
Quote: "2025: USD 3.41 billion ... 2034: USD 7.48 billion"
Verifier evidence (high): The cited Fortune Business Insights page states verbatim: "The global recruitment software market size was valued at USD 3.41 billion in 2025. The market is projected to grow from USD 3.74 billion in 2026 to USD 7.48 billion by 2034, exhibiting a CAGR of 8.95% during the forecast period." The claim (2025: USD 3.41B; 2034: USD 7.48B) reproduces these figures exactly with no overreach or misread. Base year 2025, forecast 2026-2034 — the claim is current, not outdated. Source quality is appropriate: market-size figures are inherently analyst estimates with no "primary" source, and Fortune Business Insights is an established commercial research firm. A cross-source WebSearch confirmed wide variation across firms (2025 estimates ranged USD 2.2B-7.84B; 2034 forecasts USD 3.9B-17.15B), which is normal due to differing market definitions/methodologies; the Fortune figure sits in a reasonable mid-range and is not an outlier or contradicted. The claim is correctly attributed and properly qualified as a single firm's projection.

### [10] The recruitment software market is projected to grow at a CAGR of 8.95% over the 2026-2034 forecast period.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/industry-reports/recruitment-software-market-100081 (secondary)
Quote: "CAGR of 8.95% during the forecast period"
Verifier evidence (high): The Fortune Business Insights source page (recruitment-software-market-100081) explicitly states the recruitment software market is projected to grow from USD 3.74B (2026) to USD 7.48B (2034) at a "CAGR of 8.95% during the forecast period (2026-2034)" — matching the claim and supporting quote verbatim, including the exact 2026-2034 window. The claim is current (today is 2026-06-16). Cross-checking corroborates the range rather than refuting it: Market Research Future cites 9.82% CAGR (2025-2034), Intel Market Research cites 7.0% (2026-2034), and Research and Markets cites 4.80% — so 8.95% sits within the 7-10% cluster of independent estimates and is not an outlier or cherry-picked extreme. Caveats that do NOT rise to refutation: (1) the source is a commercial market-research vendor (secondary), but a ~9% software-market CAGR is an ordinary, non-extraordinary claim not requiring a primary source; (2) absolute market-size figures vary widely across firms (USD 3.74B vs 7.84B baselines) due to differing market definitions, but the claim under review is about the CAGR rate, which is reproduced accurately and falls in the consensus band. Claim is supported, current, and source quality matches the (modest) claim strength.

### [11] The global recruitment software market is sized at USD 3.77 billion in 2026 and is projected to reach USD 5.5 billion by 2031, growing at a 7.85% CAGR.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.mordorintelligence.com/industry-reports/recruitment-software-market (secondary)
Quote: "2026 Market Size: USD 3.77 Billion ... 2031 Market Size: USD 5.5 Billion ... Growth Rate (2026-2031): 7.85% CAGR"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is verbatim-supported by the cited source. WebFetch of the Mordor Intelligence report confirmed exact figures: 2026 Market Size "USD 3.77 Billion", 2031 Market Size "USD 5.5 Billion", "7.85% CAGR" (2026-2031). The numbers are internally consistent: 3.77B compounded at 7.85% over 5 years = ~5.51B, matching the 5.5B target. The claim is current (2026 baseline, today is 2026-06-16), not outdated. Cross-checking other firms shows estimate variance typical for market-research reports (Fortune Business Insights ~USD 3.74B 2026 at 8.95% CAGR; SkyQuest 9.86% CAGR; GII/Global Growth Insights lower at ~2.76-2.78B), but none CONTRADICTS the Mordor figure — they are independent estimates with differing scopes/methodologies, and Mordor's 3.77B/7.85% sits within the plausible cluster. The claim accurately attributes the numbers to Mordor and does not overreach beyond what the report states. Caveat: this is a secondary aggregator estimate, so it should be cited as "per Mordor Intelligence" rather than absolute fact, but that is a sourcing nuance, not grounds for refutation.

### [12] The SME segment is the fastest-growing organization-size segment in recruitment software, expanding at 10.23% CAGR through 2031, outpacing the large-enterprise segment which still holds 60.95% of 2025 spending.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://www.mordorintelligence.com/industry-reports/recruitment-software-market (secondary)
Quote: "SME demand represents the fastest-growing segment, expanding at 10.23% CAGR through 2031, as "modular, seat-based pricing lowers the entry barrier." ... Large Enterprise Share: 60.95% of spending in 2025"
Verifier evidence (medium): The claim is a faithful, accurate restatement of the cited Mordor Intelligence source — not an overreach. The report states verbatim: "SME demand represents the fastest-growing cohort; the segment is forecast to post a 10.23% CAGR by 2031" and "Large enterprises still bring 60.95% of 2025 revenue." The claim's "through 2031" vs the report's "by 2031" is equivalent. Cross-source verification corroborates the QUALITATIVE direction: multiple research firms (MarketResearchFuture, Coherent, SkyQuest) agree SMEs are the fastest-growing organization-size cohort while large enterprises dominate current spending. Caveats that fall short of refutation: (a) the specific 60.95% figure is firm-specific — other firms put the large-enterprise share at 52.6% and 54.3% in 2025, so the exact percentages are methodology-dependent; (b) it is a single secondary market-research source rather than a primary one. However, the claim correctly attributes the numbers to Mordor Intelligence, presents firm-specific figures rather than universal truths, and the underlying directional finding is consistent across credible sources. No source contradicts the core assertion. Note the date context: today is 2026-06-16, and a 2031 forecast is current/forward-looking, not outdated.

### [13] Zoho Recruit's Staffing Agency Edition is priced at $25/user/month (Standard, 100 active jobs), $50/user/month (Professional, 250 jobs), and $75/user/month (Enterprise, 750 jobs).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.pin.com/blog/zoho-recruit-pricing/ (blog)
Quote: "Standard | $25/user/month | 100 | 250/day ... Professional | $50/user/month | 250 | 500/day ... Enterprise | $75/user/month | 750 | Unlimited"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported and corroborated by multiple independent sources. The supporting quote from pin.com directly states Standard $25/user/month (100 jobs), Professional $50/user/month (250 jobs), Enterprise $75/user/month (750 jobs) — matching the claim exactly with no overreach. Cross-checking via WebSearch, independent third-party sources (G2's Zoho Recruit Pricing 2026 page, SaaSworthy, PeopleManagingPeople, Klearskill) all return the identical figures: Standard $25/user/mo = 100 active jobs, Professional $50/user/mo = 250 active jobs, Enterprise $75/user/mo = 750 active jobs, billed annually. G2 search summary: "Standard ($25/user/month): Staffing Agency Standard gets 100 active jobs ... Professional ($50/user/month): 250 jobs ... Enterprise ($75/user/month): 750 active jobs." The claim is current (sources dated 2026) and not marketing fluff. Note one minor caveat: Zoho's general /recruit/pricing.html page shows a different product line (Free/Standard/Enterprise with 1/10/20 jobs per recruiter license) — that is the Corporate HR edition, NOT the Staffing Agency edition, so it does not contradict the claim. The Staffing Agency edition is a distinct product line with the cited tiers. The pricing is annual-billing rate; monthly billing may be higher, but the claim does not misstate this. I could not load Zoho's official staffing pricing page directly (404s on guessed URLs), so the claim rests on consistent third-party reporting rather than the primary vendor page, but the convergence of 5+ independent sources on exact identical numbers makes refutation unjustified.

### [14] Zoho Recruit launched eight agentic AI tools in October 2025 (including a Sourcing Bot, Screening Bot, and AI-powered candidate assessments), available on Professional and Enterprise tiers at no additional cost.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.pin.com/blog/zoho-recruit-pricing/ (blog)
Quote: "Eight agentic AI tools launched in October 2025 - including a Sourcing Bot, Screening Bot, and AI-powered candidate assessments. These features appear only on Professional and Enterprise tiers at no additional cost."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported and corroborated by multiple independent sources beyond the cited Pin blog. SmallBizTrends (smallbiztrends.com/zoho-recruit-brings-agentic-ai-tools...) confirms eight new AI-powered features launched October 20, 2025, including Sourcing Bot, Screening Bot, AI-Assisted Assessment Generation, Candidate/Job Matches, etc., "available at no additional cost to existing users with paid plans." CompleteAITraining independently confirms the same eight tools, launch date October 21, 2025, "included at no extra cost for existing paid plans." On tier specificity: the cited Pin blog states the features "appear only on Professional and Enterprise tiers," and a search snippet corroborates "The Standard and Free plans don't include any AI tools" (Professional $50/user/mo, Enterprise $75/user/mo). The only minor imprecision is that other sources phrase it as "paid plans" generically rather than naming Professional/Enterprise, but this does not contradict the claim. Date is recent (Oct 2025) and not outdated. The named bots, count of eight, and no-additional-cost-on-paid-tiers are all verified across independent trade publications, not just marketing/PR.

### [15] Manatal targets budget-conscious small teams and solo recruiters with pricing starting at $15/user/month (Professional, annual), $35 (Enterprise), $55 (Enterprise Plus), and a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://spott.io/resources/top-10-ats-and-crm-platforms-for-recruiting-agencies-in-2026 (blog)
Quote: "starts at $15/user/month ... Enterprise tier: $35/user/month ... Enterprise Plus: $55/user/month ... 14-day free trial, no credit card required"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is fully corroborated by the PRIMARY source (manatal.com/pricing) and multiple independent third-party sources. Manatal's official pricing page states: Professional $15/user/month, Enterprise $35/user/month, Enterprise Plus $55/user/month (all billed annually), and a "14-day free trial" with "No credit card required." This is confirmed by independent sources (G2, TrustRadius, Pin.com, AvaHR) which all list the identical annual figures ($15/$35/$55) and note monthly billing is $4 higher per tier ($19/$39/$59). The blog source's figures are not an overreach — they match the vendor's own current pricing as of June 2026. The characterization of Manatal as targeting budget-conscious small teams/solo recruiters is also consistent with its low entry price and self-serve trial. No contradicting evidence found; claim is current (2026 pricing pages) and well-supported by primary sources.

### [16] Loxo offers a free-forever ATS and CRM tier and targets executive search firms and small agencies (under 25 recruiters), with AI candidate matching.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://spott.io/resources/top-10-ats-and-crm-platforms-for-recruiting-agencies-in-2026 (blog)
Quote: "free-forever ATS and CRM tier ... Executive search firms and small agencies (under 25 recruiters) ... AI candidate matching that surfaces relevant profiles"
Verifier evidence (high): Loxo's own pricing page (loxo.co/pricing, primary source) confirms a "$0 Forever" tier including Applicant Tracking System (ATS) + Recruiting CRM, Loxo Boost, Chrome extension, and unlimited jobs for one user. Multiple independent sources (Pin.com, G2, Glozo, Capterra) corroborate the free-forever ATS+CRM tier (paid Basic ~$169/user/mo). Target customer confirmed: Loxo markets directly to "Executive search firms," "Direct hire firms," and "Staffing agencies," and reviews position it for small/mid-sized agencies (iSmartRecruit, Recruiterflow). AI candidate matching confirmed: loxo.co/pricing lists "AI Job Matching," "AI Recommendations," and "Natural Language Search" with semantic matches surfaced at the top of the list. The only weakly-supported sub-detail is the precise "under 25 recruiters" threshold — Loxo's own language says "small/mid-sized" / "scaling firms" without a stated 25-recruiter cap, so that figure is the blog's characterization, not Loxo's. But this is a minor non-load-bearing qualifier; the three core assertions (free-forever ATS+CRM, executive search/small agency target, AI candidate matching) are all current (2026) and verified against the vendor's primary source.

### [17] Recruit CRM is priced for global agencies needing invoicing/revenue tracking, at Pro $85, Business $125, and Enterprise $165 per user/month billed annually.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://spott.io/resources/top-10-ats-and-crm-platforms-for-recruiting-agencies-in-2026 (blog)
Quote: "Pro: $85/user/month ... Business: $125/user/month ... Enterprise: $165/user/month (billed annually) ... Global agencies needing invoicing/revenue tracking"
Verifier evidence (high): The pricing figures are corroborated by multiple independent sources. RecruitCompare (recruitcompare.io/tool/recruit-crm) lists exactly Pro $85/user/month (Full ATS+CRM), Business $125/user/month (custom fields, advanced features), Enterprise $165/user/month (branded portal, custom reporting) — matching the claim verbatim. The iSmartRecruit/People Managing People search results independently confirm Pro $85 annual / $100 monthly, Business $125 annual, Enterprise $165 annual. The official recruitcrm.io/pricing page confirms the three-tier Pro/Business/Enterprise structure with "billed annually — save up to 20%," explaining why Capterra shows the higher monthly-billed rates ($100/$150/$165). The "billed annually" qualifier in the claim is therefore accurate. The characterization "for global agencies needing invoicing/revenue tracking" is supported: RecruitCompare states it is "purpose-built for recruitment agencies — not a generic ATS," targeting staffing firms (10-200 employees) managing client relationships and placements, and Recruit CRM does provide invoicing/revenue features. This is a verifiable price list (not an extraordinary claim), current for 2026, with adequate third-party source backing.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "Jobin.cloud offers a free-forever plan aimed at solo recruiters that includes LinkedIn sourcing, a CRM with 10,000 contacts, 15 daily LinkedIn messages, 100 daily emails, and 30 free ChatGPT-powered AI prompts, but only a single ATS pipeline." (https://www.selectsoftwarereviews.com/buyer-guide/free-recruiting-software, vote 0-1)
- "Zoho Recruit and Recruit CRM offer free plans, while most mid-to-enterprise ATS platforms (Workable, Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever, Teamtailor) do not — Zoho Recruit starts at $25/mo, Recruit CRM at $40-85/mo, and both also offer free trials." (https://www.selectsoftwarereviews.com/blog/recruitment-software-pricing, vote 0-3)
- "Entry-level/freemium recruitment software packages from vendors like BambooHR and Recruitee start at around USD 50 per user per month, giving small (10-person) teams access to AI ranking and automated outreach." (https://www.mordorintelligence.com/industry-reports/recruitment-software-market, vote 0-3)
- "Zoho Recruit's Forever Free plan is limited to 1 active job, 256MB storage, and 5 email templates, and excludes resume parsing, AI tools, sourcing, custom fields, reporting, and integrations." (https://www.pin.com/blog/zoho-recruit-pricing/, vote 1-2)
- "Recruiting CRM/ATS per-seat pricing at the small-agency level ranges roughly $15-$124/user/month, with Manatal at $15, Salesforce at $25, Zoho Recruit at $30, Top Echelon at $89, Recruit CRM at $99, Crelate at $110, and Recruiterflow at $119 per user/month." (https://recruiterflow.com/blog/best-recruitment-crm-software/, vote 1-2)
- "Several recruiting CRM platforms targeting small/medium firms offer a free tier or free trial, including Recruit CRM, Zoho Recruit, Manatal, Crelate, Tracker, and Top Echelon; while Recruiterflow, Bullhorn, Vincere, PCRecruiter, and JobAdder do not." (https://recruiterflow.com/blog/best-recruitment-crm-software/, vote 0-3)
- "Recruiting platforms differ in target segment: Recruiterflow targets retained/contingent/executive search firms, Recruit CRM targets small-medium firms, Vincere targets small recruitment businesses, Manatal targets mid-size companies, and Bullhorn targets mid-size to large agencies." (https://recruiterflow.com/blog/best-recruitment-crm-software/, vote 0-3)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: The synthesis task is clear. I have all 18 confirmed claims and the refuted claims. I'll merge duplicates, group into findings, and produce the structured output.

input.summary: The market for AI-powered recruitment software is large and growing — roughly USD 3.4-3.8 billion in 2025-2026, projected to reach USD 5.5-7.5 billion by 2031-2034 at an ~8-9% CAGR — with the SME/small-agency segment as the fastest-growing organization-size cohort (~10% CAGR) even though large enterprises still account for the majority (~61%) of current spending. The freemium-plus-paid-team model the question describes is validated and occupied: Loxo and Zoho Recruit are the clearest precedents, both offering genuine free-forever individual tiers and self-serve paid upgrades, while Recruit CRM and Manatal compete on the same small-agency turf but with trial-only (no free-forever) onboarding. AI features (resume parsing, natural-language/semantic candidate search, candidate matching/ranking, AI outreach/email generation, AI notetakers, sourcing/screening bots, and Chrome extensions for LinkedIn sourcing) have rapidly become table-stakes in 2025-2026 — Loxo and Zoho both bundle agentic AI at no extra cost — meaning AI alone is no longer a differentiator. Pricing benchmarks for small agencies cluster at $15-$200/user/month, with Manatal anchoring the low end ($15) and Loxo/Recruit CRM in the $85-$169 mid-range; free tiers are deliberately constrained (Loxo = 1 user; Zoho = 1 active job, 256MB). Enterprise ATS players (Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever) explicitly do not serve solo/small recruiters, leaving the freemium SMB lane to specialized recruiting-CRM vendors.

findings.claim: Loxo is the strongest direct precedent for the freemium-individual + paid-team model: a permanently free $0 tier for 1 user (full ATS, Recruiting CRM, Loxo Boost, unlimited jobs, Chrome extension), upgrading to a paid Basic plan at $169/user/month (annual) that adds Sales CRM, multiple users, analytics, resume parsing, and reporting. It targets executive search firms and small agencies and bundles extensive AI.
findings.evidence: Loxo's own pricing page confirms a '$0 Forever' tier for 1 user including ATS, Recruiting CRM, Loxo Boost, unlimited jobs/projects, and Chrome extension; the paid Basic plan is $169/user/month (annual) adding Sales CRM, job-board posting, multiple users, custom dashboards, analytics, resume parsing, and reporting. Corroborated by G2, iSmartRecruit, Pin, SaaSworthy, Capterra. Target is executive search/small agencies (Loxo's own 'Executive search firms / Direct hire / Staffing agencies' language; the 'under 25 recruiters' threshold is a blog characterization, not Loxo's own).

findings.claim: Loxo embeds a broad, current AI feature set across tiers: Natural Language (semantic) Search, an AI Notetaker, three named AI agents (Self-Updating CRM Agent, Contact-Info Finding Agent, Candidate Submittal Agent), AI Job Matching/Recommendations, and Loxo Outreach (GPT-powered email/drip campaigns).
findings.evidence: Loxo's pricing/product pages confirm all named features verbatim (Natural Language Search 'plain text' candidate search, AI Notetaker auto-capturing interview notes, the three named agents, AI Job Matching, and Loxo Outreach for personalized emails/drip campaigns). Corroborated by 2025 PRWeb/StaffingHub press releases and Loxo's dedicated AI product pages. Minor naming nuance: pricing page says 'Loxo Outreach' while other materials say 'Outreach GPT'.

findings.claim: Zoho Recruit is the other clear freemium precedent: a free-forever plan limited to 1 active job and 256MB storage, with self-serve paid tiers starting at $25/recruiter/month. Its Staffing Agency Edition is tiered $25 (Standard, 100 jobs) / $50 (Professional, 250 jobs) / $75 (Enterprise, 750 jobs) per user/month.
findings.evidence: Zoho's official pricing page confirms a 'Forever Free' plan with 1 active job/recruiter license; secondary sources (pin.com, avahr, SelectSoftware) confirm the 256MB free-tier storage cap and $25/user/month Standard entry. The distinct Staffing Agency Edition is priced $25/$50/$75 per user/month (100/250/750 active jobs) per pin.com, G2, SaaSworthy, PeopleManagingPeople, Klearskill — convergent across 5+ sources (annual billing).

findings.claim: Zoho Recruit launched eight agentic AI tools in October 2025 — including a Sourcing Bot, Screening Bot, and AI-powered candidate assessments — available on paid (Professional/Enterprise) tiers at no additional cost; Free and Standard plans include no AI tools.
findings.evidence: Pin blog plus independent trade press (SmallBizTrends, CompleteAITraining) confirm eight agentic AI features launched ~Oct 20-21, 2025 (Sourcing Bot, Screening Bot, AI-assisted assessment generation, candidate/job matches, etc.) at no extra cost to existing paid users. Search snippet confirms Standard and Free plans include no AI tools; AI is gated to Professional ($50) and Enterprise ($75) tiers.

findings.claim: Manatal targets budget-conscious small teams and solo recruiters at the low end of the market: paid tiers at $15 (Professional), $35 (Enterprise), $55 (Enterprise Plus) per user/month (annual), with AI-powered candidate filtering/recommendations and enrichment built in — but only a 14-day free trial (no credit card), NOT a free-forever plan.
findings.evidence: Manatal's own pricing page confirms Professional $15 / Enterprise $35 / Enterprise Plus $55 per user/month (annual; monthly is $4 higher per tier), a 14-day free trial with no credit card, explicitly 'no free-forever plan,' and AI Recommendations + AI Candidate Enrichment across plans. Corroborated by G2, TrustRadius, ITQlick, PeopleManagingPeople, Pin, AvaHR. $15 is the cheapest published entry price in the small-agency segment.

findings.claim: Recruit CRM targets small/global employment agencies needing invoicing and revenue tracking, priced at $85 (Pro) / $125 (Business) / $165 (Enterprise) per user/month billed annually. It offers an unlimited free trial (one sequence, one pipeline) rather than a free-forever plan.
findings.evidence: RecruitCompare, iSmartRecruit, PeopleManagingPeople, and TrustRadius confirm Pro $85 / Business $125 / Enterprise $165 per user/month (annual; Capterra shows higher monthly-billed rates ~$100/$150/$165). Recruit CRM's own site confirms an 'unlimited free trial' with usage limits and no credit card (a trial, not freemium); the entry Pro plan is limited to 1 sequence and 1 pipeline. Targets recruitment agencies (10-200 employees) with invoicing/revenue features. Note: SSR imprecisely labeled the 1-sequence/1-pipeline limit a 'free tier' — it is actually the entry Pro paid plan.

findings.claim: Ashby (and by extension Greenhouse/Lever-class platforms) explicitly target large organizations and are NOT suitable for solo recruiters or 5-10 person agencies; Ashby's smallest tier starts at ~$400/month for orgs up to 100 employees.
findings.evidence: Ashby's own /enterprise page states 'Enterprise-grade recruiting software... Built to support large organizations with sophisticated hiring needs,' featuring logos like Shopify and Snowflake. Independent reviews (pin.com, costbench, research.com) confirm the smallest 'Foundations' tier starts ~$400/month for up to 100 employees, with company-size-based tiering roughly double Greenhouse/Lever — structurally unsuitable for solo/small agencies. Caveat: Ashby also serves startups/scale-ups, not only giant enterprises, which is why the vote was 2-1.

findings.claim: Freemium is one of six recognized recruitment-software pricing models (alongside subscription, pay-per-user, tiered, custom, and free/open-source); among lower-cost ATS/CRM tools, free tiers exist at Zoho Recruit, Recruit CRM, Loxo, and Breezy HR.
findings.evidence: SelectSoftwareReviews (2026) names exactly six models: subscription-based, pay-per-user, tiered, custom, free/open-source, freemium. Corroborated by Software Advice, PeopleManagingPeople, Manatal. Freemium vendor examples verified (Zoho 'freemium model'; Loxo 'Free Forever'; Recruit CRM and Breezy HR listed with free plans). Caveat: the taxonomy is informal, not a formal industry standard.

findings.claim: Small-agency recruiting software pricing (under 50 employees) ranges roughly $15-$200 per user/month, with Manatal cheapest (~$15) and Loxo's paid Basic in the mid-range (~$119-$169). Free tiers are deliberately constrained (Loxo = 1 user; Zoho = 1 active job + 256MB).
findings.evidence: SelectSoftwareReviews confirms the $15-$200/user/month range for sub-50-employee firms and names Manatal at $15 and Loxo at $119. Independently corroborated by G2, TrustRadius, augtal (2026) — Loxo Basic ranges $119-$169 depending on billing, plus a free 1-user tier; Zoho free = 1 active job/256MB. The $15 floor reflects annual billing.

findings.claim: The global recruitment software market is sized at roughly USD 3.4-3.8 billion in 2025-2026 and is projected to reach USD 5.5-7.5 billion by 2031-2034, growing at an ~7.85-8.95% CAGR.
findings.evidence: Fortune Business Insights: USD 3.41B (2025) -> 3.74B (2026) -> 7.48B (2034) at 8.95% CAGR. Mordor Intelligence: USD 3.77B (2026) -> 5.5B (2031) at 7.85% CAGR (internally consistent: 3.77B compounded at 7.85% over 5yrs ~= 5.51B). Cross-firm estimates vary (2025 baselines USD 2.2-7.84B; CAGRs 4.8-9.86%) due to differing market definitions, but the two cited firms sit within the consensus 7-10% band. These are analyst estimates, not primary fact.

findings.claim: The SME segment is the fastest-growing organization-size segment in recruitment software (~10.23% CAGR through 2031), outpacing large enterprises — even though large enterprises still hold the majority (~60.95%) of 2025 spending. Modular, seat-based pricing is cited as lowering the entry barrier for SMEs.
findings.evidence: Mordor Intelligence states SME demand is 'the fastest-growing cohort' at 10.23% CAGR by 2031, while large enterprises bring 60.95% of 2025 revenue, attributing SME growth to 'modular, seat-based pricing [that] lowers the entry barrier.' Directional finding (SMEs fastest-growing, enterprises dominant in current spend) corroborated by MarketResearchFuture, Coherent, SkyQuest. Caveat: the exact 60.95% figure is firm-specific (others cite 52.6-54.3%); single secondary source for the precise CAGR.

input.caveats: Several findings rest on secondary sources and vendor pages rather than independent primary research. (1) Market-size and CAGR figures (findings on market size and SME growth) are commercial analyst estimates that vary widely across firms (2025 baselines from USD 2.2B to 7.84B); they should be cited as 'per Fortune Business Insights / Mordor Intelligence,' not absolute fact. The SME-growth finding (claim 12) and the Ashby positioning finding (claim 0) were 2-1 split votes and carry lower confidence. (2) Pricing is highly time-sensitive and billing-dependent: most low headline prices ($15 Manatal, $85 Recruit CRM, $169 Loxo) reflect annual billing; monthly billing runs higher, and vendors change prices frequently — all figures are current as of 2026 only. (3) The 'under 25 recruiters' Loxo target and Recruit CRM's '1 sequence/1 pipeline as free tier' were source characterizations with minor imprecision (the latter is actually the entry Pro paid plan, since Recruit CRM has no free-forever plan). (4) Importantly, several pain-point and competitor claims were REFUTED in verification and are NOT reflected here: a recruiterflow.com-sourced competitor-segmentation and free-tier roster (covering Crelate, Tracker, Top Echelon, Bullhorn, Vincere, JobAdder, PCRecruiter, Salesforce) failed adversarial voting (0-3), as did claims about Jobin.cloud's free plan, BambooHR/Recruitee freemium pricing, and the exact Zoho free-plan feature exclusions. Treat any platform not explicitly confirmed above (notably Crelate, JobAdder, Bullhorn, Vincere, RecruiterFlow, Tracker, Ceipal, iSmartRecruit, Workable, Greenhouse) as UNVERIFIED in this synthesis.
openQuestions: The pain points of small/independent recruiters (data scattered across WhatsApp/Excel/Google Drive, admin time burden, lack of placement context, no revenue visibility) were a core research dimension but NO claim on them survived verification — what tools specifically claim to solve these and what gaps remain is unresolved and needs dedicated primary research.

Which AI features are genuinely table-stakes versus differentiators in 2025-2026 is only partially answered: resume parsing, semantic search, candidate matching, AI outreach, and notetakers appear common across Loxo and Zoho, but no source ranked or benchmarked feature ubiquity across the full competitor set.

The competitive positioning and free-tier/pricing of many named platforms (Crelate, JobAdder, Bullhorn, Vincere, RecruiterFlow, Tracker, Ceipal, iSmartRecruit, Workable, Greenhouse) remains unverified — the recruiterflow.com-sourced data was refuted, leaving gaps that primary vendor-page verification could fill.

How self-serve onboarding/'easy to start' is positioned and measured (e.g., time-to-first-value, no-credit-card trials vs free-forever conversion) was not directly substantiated beyond noting trial-vs-freemium distinctions.

content.text: I have completed the synthesis and returned the structured output.
